title: GenBen:A Genarative Benchmark for LLM-Aided Design

reviews.summary: This paper presents GenBen, a generative benchmark for evaluating LLM-aided hardware design that goes beyond prior RTL code-generation benchmarks by (i) constructing a diverse, difficulty-tiered test suite spanning knowledge, coding, debugging, and multimodal tasks; (ii) integrating QoR-aware metrics (synthesizability, power, area, timing) using an open-source toolchain (Icarus Verilog, OpenLane, Sky130 PDK); and (iii) attempting to mitigate test-set contamination through static and dynamic perturbations. The authors evaluate nine LLMs/MLLMs with a standardized prompt and pass@5 protocol, report performance across task categories and difficulty tiers, and provide an initial QoR analysis for top models.
strengths: The benchmark explicitly couples simulation-level correctness with ASIC-oriented QoR metrics (PPA and timing) via a fully open-source flow (Icarus, Yosys/OpenLane, Sky130). This end-to-end framing is valuable and underexplored in many RTL benchmarks.
The perturbation pipeline (static and dynamic) aims to reduce data contamination and provide robustness testing, addressing a real challenge for open benchmarks.
Inclusion of debugging tasks and multimodal inputs (diagrams/schematics/waveforms) better reflects real design workflows than code-only benchmarks.
Difficulty tiering across tasks offers a principled way to detect progress and stratify failure modes.
Broad model coverage (6 MLLMs and 3 LLMs), standardized prompting, and pass@5 evaluation help reduce confounders.
The end-to-end testing infrastructure and automation for lint/sim/synthesis/STA is a strong reproducibility feature.
The overall benchmark architecture and workflow are clear: dataset curation, testbench enhancement, perturbation design, and evaluation pipeline are reasonably described.
The motivation is well-articulated, especially the need for QoR-aware evaluation and coverage-improved testbenches.

A public, open, reproducible, QoR-aware benchmark can materially advance LAD research by aligning metrics with silicon feasibility and surfacing differences between functional correctness and synthesizability.
The attempt to systematize contamination mitigation and robustness via perturbations is timely and useful for the community.
weaknesses: The claims around verification coverage (e.g., “Worst Coverage Score 95.17%” in Table 1) are implausibly strong without detailed, problem-level metrics and methodology; it is unclear whether these refer to line/toggle/functional coverage, how they are measured, and on which tools/configurations.

The contamination-mitigation approach (script-based generation and perturbations) is sensible but not validated quantitatively; no near-duplicate or memorization audits (e.g., n-gram shingling, MinHash, dataset overlap checks) are reported.
The dynamic perturbation methodology is under-specified (e.g., if/how LLMs generate the perturbations, how seeds/versions are controlled, and how fairness across models is ensured).
QoR normalization is mentioned but the exact scoring and reference baselines are not fully specified; without per-problem normalization formulas and thresholds, interpretability is limited.

Dataset size and distribution are inconsistent: the paper states “300 tests,” while Table 3 sums to 360. The multimodal set size and image preparation (resolution, annotation standards) are not detailed.

The reported results contain inconsistencies across figures/tables (e.g., different values for similar categories in heatmaps vs. bar charts, occasional mislabeled axes like “Synatx”/“Synthesizbility”), making it hard to trust exact numbers.

While the paper emphasizes testbench coverage improvements, concrete coverage statistics per testbench (line/toggle/functional/bin coverage) and a comparison against baselines (e.g., VerilogEval/RTLLM) are not provided.
The QoR case study (Figure 15) is illustrative but anecdotal; without per-task QoR deltas, variance, and synthesis logs, it is difficult to generalize conclusions.
Multiple formatting artifacts and typos in figures/tables obscure some results; several charts repeat labels or contain placeholder-like entries.

The definition of “Pass Rate (PR)” is formalized, but how it aggregates across task types (binary pass/fail vs. graded metrics, penalties for warnings, etc.) is not consistently connected to reported plots and tables.

Recent resource- or QoR-aware benchmarks and frameworks are not discussed or compared, such as Pluto (Pareto-aware QoR benchmarking with expert-optimized references) and ResBench (FPGA resource-aware evaluation). These are highly relevant and would contextualize GenBen’s QoR scope and novelty.
Agentic frameworks like CorrectHDL that combine LLMs with HLS/EDA feedback for improved PPA are not contrasted in terms of goals, methodology, or evaluation metrics.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you provide rigorous, per-testbench coverage statistics (line/toggle/functional, coverage goals, closure criteria) and clarify the “95.17% Worst Coverage Score” claim in Table 1? What tool/metric produced that number?
2. How large is the final dataset exactly (counts per category and difficulty), and why does the text (≈300 tests) not match Table 3 (summing to 360)? Please reconcile and release a manifest.
3. How are dynamic perturbations generated and controlled at evaluation time? Are the same perturbed instances used for all models in a run? Will you release seeds and the perturbation generator to ensure reproducibility and fairness?
4. How do you detect/quantify potential training-set contamination or near-duplicates (e.g., between GenBen and public corpora/benchmarks)? Have you run any leakage audits (e.g., shingling/MinHash analyses, memorization probes)?
5. Please formalize the QoR normalization and scoring. What is the exact formula, baseline choice per problem, and how are multi-metric outcomes (area/power/timing) aggregated into final scores?
6. For multimodal tests, what is the image preparation pipeline (formats, resolutions, annotations)? Are all models given identical image inputs, and are any OCR/diagram-specific prompts used?
7. How are syntax penalties applied in aggregate scoring (e.g., “5% penalty per warning, min 60%”)? Does this interact with functional and synthesizability scores, and how is the final PR computed across mixed binary/continuous metrics?
8. Will you release per-problem synthesis/STA reports and consolidated CSVs of all metrics to enable downstream analysis and meta-benchmarking (e.g., task difficulty vs. QoR trade-offs)?
reviews.assessment: GenBen addresses an important gap by proposing an open, end-to-end, QoR-aware benchmark for LLM-aided hardware design, with explicit attention to verification coverage, multimodality, debugging, and contamination mitigation. The design choices and infrastructure are promising and, if solidified, could significantly benefit the community. However, the present submission has notable shortcomings: key methodological pieces are under-specified (coverage measurement, contamination audits, dynamic perturbation governance, QoR normalization), several dataset/result inconsistencies undermine confidence, and comparisons to recent QoR/resource-aware work (e.g., Pluto, ResBench) are missing. I view GenBen as a potentially impactful resource once the above issues are addressed. I encourage the authors to (i) rigorously document and release coverage/QoR artifacts and perturbation generators with seeds; (ii) reconcile dataset counts and clean up result inconsistencies; and (iii) position GenBen against Pluto/ResBench with, ideally, partial quantitative cross-checks. With these improvements, the work would merit acceptance; in its current form, it falls short of ICLR standards for completeness and rigor.
